If you’ve been building Angular apps long enough, you already know that pipes are not just about formatting text or changing dates. Pipes can be a performance optimization tool, a UI logic simplifier, and a declarative transformation engine — if you know how to use them correctly.
But here’s the secret:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most developers understand only what pipes do.
Senior developers understand when, why, and how to use them strategically.
In this article, we’ll go deep into 7 advanced pipe techniques that separate intermediate Angular developers from real seniors. You’ll not only learn how to write pipes you’ll understand how they impact your architecture, readability, and performance.
1. Pure vs Impure Pipes — The Difference That Matters
At first glance, a pipe looks simple — it just transforms the input.
But behind the scenes, Angular determines how often that transformation runs.
By default, pipes are pure, meaning:

• They run only when the reference of the input changes, not on every event.
This makes them efficient.
Example of a Pure Pipe:
@Pipe({
name…
